Halbert Interview Crib Notes
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 13 --

Halbert Interview Crib Notes
www.halbertology.com 2
Halbert Interview Crib Notes
Christine: Hi, today we're sitting down with Bond and Kevin Halbert, the second generation of one of
the greatest copy and marketing dynasties the world has ever seen to talk a little bit about
Halbertology. Bond, Kevin, thank you so much for meeting with us today. I am sure that all
of the fans are going to be really excited to hear about everything that you have to tell us.
Bond: Thanks for having us.
Christine: Let's get started shall we? Can you tell me how being in business for yourselves in
copywriting and marketing has transformed your lives?
Bond: I tried working 9 to 5. I just can't do it. Going to the same place and I find seeing the same
people, we're going to the same places, seeing the same four walls, and doing the same
thing over and over again gets really tedious on me. It really just didn't work out very well.
One of the things, doing this on our own and taking control of your own destiny has
allowed me to do is it gives you a freedom that a lot of people talk about. But I'm kind of
like a living example of it. My kids have not really spent a lot of time with babysitters. I
never missed their plays. My retired mom wants to go out to lunch. I'm free to do so. If I
see a movie I want to go see, I go check it out on Monday morning. The freedom of
controlling the schedule and being able to do what I want means the world to me.
Kevin: I like the idea that I get to work at night and I get to choose where I'm going to go. I always
go sit in the coffee house somewhere because it gets me into a mode and I just get to sit
there. I choose my coffee houses by how late they're open. That's my commute, if I decide
to go to a coffee house, or if I want to work at home or whatever. We get to trade off our
time. We get to trade off do we want more money, do we want to put more time, or do
we want to relax more and just enjoy.
Bond: When you're working 9 to 5 you can't just take off to go spend time with loved ones who
are in the hospital. You can't just say yes, I'll be there no matter what to people who want
to [Unintelligible 00:02:32]. The freedom for me has meant an incredible amount.
Kevin: If I have volleyball coming up I can go, "I want to go play volleyball today." And I have to
do that during the day, so I'll just go do that.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 13 --

Halbert Interview Crib Notes
www.halbertology.com 3
Bond: Yeah. We're two different animals.
Christine: It's easy to see how you were confident that you would succeed, but not everyone has the
Prince of Print for a dad. Do you think anyone can do what you do and why?
Bond: I certainly do. I've seen a bunch of people do it. Our father was an extremely pedantic guy,
he just loved to teach. He loved to teach his craft, he'd done it to us. As soon as he learned
something he wanted to teach us. We were with him and paying very close attention. I
always tell people he quit his last job. He was fired the day before I was born. We grew up
with him always being an independent, self-made person. But we were very cognizant or
very much aware when he started teaching everybody else. In fact, the whole guru status
kind of boils back down to his birthday when he sat down and wrote the very first blog
which was his plan. Starting off and writing his newsletter which was his plan, starting off
and writing his newsletter and then teaching his craft to other people. By that time he was
already teaching Kevin and I the business.
And so we sat around and watched him not only train tons of people but the guys who are
everybody are saying they're the biggest and the best in the business of things. We
watched him train them. It's not just a one-way street, our father would learn from them
the same way we learn from other copywriters and marketers that we work with. If
there's anything I'm 100% sure of is that anybody can do it because I've watched a whole
bunch of people come in very green. Here's the key thing, they started in what they were
doing and they kept at it. And those that started and kept at it really did do it. There was
no defining... That guy's got that star quality and he's going to make it in this business. If
they put in the time, they put in the work, they put in the effort, they did it.
Kevin: I think the fact that we have all the success stories from people that have just learned
from what he's left behind, seminars and stuff like that. There's always massive success
stories from people who never met or knew him, and never had to be around and the
extra time that we did. Of course we got something more out of being around him. But
the thing is that is his concepts and everything he teaches... All of his stuff I still go through
to this day over and over again. And I get something deeper out of the same thing I
already know because I went through it again, and it keeps going like that.
Bond: Some of the guys went on to become hired guns, highest paid copywriters around. Other
guys put their copy only to use selling their own wares. Whereas other people took the
lessons and understood the key concepts behind the work a lot greater than just
copywriting.
Kevin: Anyone can do it. He wrote The Boron Letters so that if he wasn't there someone could
learn to do it just from the letters that he wrote.
Bond: Marketing has anything to do with human relations when you want another human to do
something. If you're trying to get your kid to do your homework, it's marketing. AIDA,
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 13 --

Halbert Interview Crib Notes
www.halbertology.com 4
attention, interest, desire, and action. If you learn the core principles behind every single
strategy and technique then you just have a lot of power that goes far beyond just
copywriting. And it's of course useful in every aspect of marketing. He was teaching us...
When I was maybe between 9 and 11, Kevin was 18 months older than I am, so we've
been learning a long time even before that. It's classic. I said thank God dad went to prison
because if it weren't for that I wouldn't have the proof that we had this incredible
education at an early age. Because you think, his dad's a neurosurgeon. It's doesn't mean
anything that he's got skills. But it was different in our case. Like I said our father just
loved to teach and he loved to teach us.
Christine: What's a regular day like for you?
Kevin: One of the things I noticed about other writers, copywriters, and marketers is sometimes
you have a ritual of getting ready. You do these things. You get your coffee, put it on a
table and you do whatever because it lets your mind get in that mental mode to start
working. But when I get into the emails one of the things I like to make sure that anybody
writes us or doesn't think that I don't take care of them in the afternoon. If they have a
problem overnight I want them taken care of it right away. What I'm doing is I'm freeing
myself for the rest of the day. I have to wonder if somebody's got a problem I need to take
care of. Once I check my emails, fix everybody's things if there is anything. And then I'm
into my routine of doing what I want to do for the rest of the day. And that's just going
what promotion do we want to work on, what's the biggest step that's in the way. Or
what do I have to get going that has to be going while I'm getting the other pieces all set
up.
And so he rings me when he can finally ring me at 10 am and then we make our game
plan. We go what are we trying to accomplish, what are the things we need to do, what
can you do, what can I do, and what will get it done. So we just attack it that way. It's
usually a no-brainer because we just figure out, "Oh, you've got the skill set for that, and
you got the skill set for that." So we know what we're expecting of each other when we
come back around and go, "The things get done, what's in the way. How do we take that?"
Everyone just wants to check in to make sure that we're still on the same page and know
each other where each other is on the task that we're trying to accomplish.
Bond: I'm the exact opposite. I actually do all my creative work in the beginning of the day. I get
up early. I usually wake up between 5 and 5:30 am and without an alarm clock. I don't
honestly know if I own an alarm clock. What I found most copywriters, marketers, and
creatives do is they carve out that time that's quiet time for them. For me, that happens
to be in the morning because my brother's asleep, my wife and kids have gone off to do
their lives and so forth. The other thing that's common with almost all copywriters and
marketers is that routine he's talking about. It's critical because it's the only way that you
can rely on exactly this is when my brain is going to be hitting on all cylinders. You quickly
learn when you have to perform creatively. When you have to come up with a good idea
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 13 --

Halbert Interview Crib Notes
www.halbertology.com 5
you have to be responsive. You very quickly learn that we as humans are such products of
our environment. In fact, Kevin mentioned something that's kind of like key hack which
just shows and approves this. My dad taught me this too. He said, "If you ever head in a
funk and you just can't get into the mood to work go to a research library." We're such
social creatures it's impossible to be in a research library with a bunch of people working
and not all of a sudden get into the mood to work. He goes to a coffee house where
there's a ton of people reading and doing homework, writing reports and scripts for their
plays, and stuff like that. What happens is he automatically will get into the vibe and the
mood to work. When you go beyond that you finally start to realize if I have a little too
much coffee it changes the way I think. You start to really learn that your brain is a muscle
that needs input, nurture, rest, creative input, it needs all kinds of stuff.
And so every single top copywriter I know, and even every single creative top marketer I
know, the people who are the copywriters, they get into a routine that nails it down so
that they can be sure that they're going to be on fire. If you want to make sure that you
have me in my prime, it's between Monday and Thursday, and it's between 8 and 10
o'clock in the morning. That's prime time. If you catch me at that time, sometimes people
would ask me for an interview and I just say this is the time that I prefer and so forth
because I know that I'm going to be on. I'm so aware of my schedule. I know when the
office is going to be too noisy. I know because that affects filming. I know what not to eat
the night before and I know that I need a certain amount... If I don't get out, exercise, and
get enough outside air time and sunlight on my face, and all this stuff... It comes down to
a very boiled down routine.
Christine: Many of the greatest names in copywriting and marketing have come together in honor of
your dad. What does having this lineup mean to you?
Kevin: If you're going to be really honest about it, what can we put together, what will these
people want. We do a survey and we say what can we deliver them. We'd already done
The Gary Halbert Letters All-Star Audio Series. We had that mistake of the audio series
where we invited a bunch of people expecting half of them to flake and none of them
dropped out. And so we ended up making a double album on the first output. So we
ended up with 12 people and then we did another album with another 6 or 7 people. The
same accident kind of happened. We're like, who do we want on this project? Who can
give these people what they want. When they're done going through who's going to give
them stuff that will make their lives better and will actually have an impact and effect if
they execute. So we got through that dream stage and the same thing happened. We
thought about inviting a few people. We invited a lot of people and they all said yes. It got
to the point where we felt very honored that they respect our dad that much.
It looked pretty much and it's turned out so far that everyone we asked said yes. And then
we can give these people anything they want because they're all important. The question
becomes we can get all these great people but we can have anybody. I mean that in a
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 13 --

Halbert Interview Crib Notes
www.halbertology.com 6
good way. What can we put together that will be the best for everybody, for all the
audience? And the thing was part of this was a little selfish motivation. Part of selfish
motivation is, we're kind of fans of these people and these guys are kind of big experts.
When it comes to the 2.0 era these people who respect our father so much kind of further
our education. A part of it was me getting what I want and knowing that what I want out
of learning this group together to make this big, giant thing, which wasn't really originally
planned to be a big, giant thing is we put in here that I want to see because I'm the same
as the people we're going to provide all this stuff for, that we got out of the survey.
Bond: For me the first initial reaction was I felt really blessed. I picked up the phone and started
calling, inviting people for the All-Star Audio Series and then one day all of a suddenly I'm
talking to big names in the business that everybody else is paying a lot of money to get
access to and so forth. They're picking up the phone and answering in honor of our father
and what he's done. But what makes it even better for the audience that's really great
about this, and Kevin's alluding to it, is these are all Gary Halbert fans. The one thing that
Gary Halbert fans did is they take, like I was talking about those core strategies. And when
they take it they're applying this to the areas that we don't work in. They're applying it to
podcasting, traffic generation, YouTube channels, PPC, and all these different kinds of
places.
There are two things about this. One is they're really well-named, recognized experts and
that's more than just star power. What that is, is that security and knowing that you're
learning what's right. The difference between asking somebody, "Hey, what's a good way
to write this or what's a good way to do this?" And hearing a whole... How do I write a good
short story? When you're getting the answer from Stephen King, it's not just that Stephen
King's a great writer, it's that you know he's a good writer. You could be in a room with a
bunch of strangers and you're not sure which one's a good writer and all kind of giving you
slightly adjusted advice. It brings a level of expertise that you can rely on. That's what
branding is. Branding is when people know by the name that they know they're getting
something that's of quality. These are all high quality marketers. That's a big benefit and it's
a big benefit to us. As Kevin was saying, we're going to be students here for a lot of this.
When it comes to the copywriting and it comes to a lot of the marketing we're going to be
in there giving answers, presenting and so forth. When it comes to some of the stuff these
people are doing we're going to be taking notes just like anybody else would. But the
advantage that these guys are going to bring and show us is part of connection to the Gary
Halbert teachings. Because when you have Gary Halbert teachings and you go into
something like PPC you get an automatic advantage in knowing how this is the image and
this are the words that are going to go right to the core of what this person truly wants and
desires, and that emotional trigger that's going to make them buy, that's going to make
them follow your call to action, and do what it is you want to do.
Christine: This is the one everybody's dying to know. What was it like growing up as a son of Gary
Halbert?
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 13 --

Halbert Interview Crib Notes
www.halbertology.com 7
Bond: It was different. I can tell you that being Gary Halbert's son was not the normal experience.
Imagine having a father whose brain can't figure out anything that's not related to
persuading another human being. It can't drive down the street. It can't make you breakfast,
literally. Our father could not make us breakfast if we wanted to. If mom went away or is
out of town and there wasn't a housekeeper. We were eating stuff out of a liquor store in
Santa Fe. He really was this guy whose brain was like 95% of every gray cell in his brain was
only working on persuasion, marketing, and copywriting, and there was nothing else to it.
His insane focus is unparalleled. You could not do it. You couldn't be a Gary Halbert and
match that, and I'm not willing to do it, unless you...
Kevin: You have a price to pay.
Bond: Yeah. You have to give up caring about everything except for that, for 30 years. The focus
and dedication was so intense.
Kevin: It's almost like an affliction.
Bond: Then there was the aspects of his personality which was my father was always messing with
people. The only thing he did... If he was in a room with you and you're just hanging out it
was compelling for him to try and push your buttons, trying to make you happy, trying to
make you upset, trying to motivate you to do something, or whatever. What he was doing
was social experimentation. He was messing with people all the time trying to figure out
where these hot buttons were, what makes you react. Because a lot of people think
copywriting is about fancy writing, creativity, and being really good with words. It does help.
There is some wordsmithing involved and there's some talent, and our father had an
incredible vocabulary that you wouldn't have recognized if you just read his copy. But it all
boils down to human psychology. What you say was more important than how you said it.
He practiced that a lot. Imagine every dinner conversation started with clickbait. Every
conversation starts off with something like, "Hey, I just figured out a way to make $20
million.” And he's waiting for us to go, "Really, how?"
Kevin: I used to resist it, he said that was cool. I would take the bait.
Bond: But our father was doing that all the time. He did with us. He did it with his friends and
everything. So we saw it constantly. There was time when it was a little embarrassing to be
growing up Halbert.
Kevin: Yeah, it's excruciating.
Bond: Until you got into the groove of it and you start to enjoy it like I did and all of a sudden I'm
torturing Kevin as much as my dad did with embarrassment. But the other thing was the
way that he thought was a little bit different. It was a little bit more hardcore. You know
how a comedian will point out something that's just the ridiculousness of the situation, he
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 13 --

Halbert Interview Crib Notes
www.halbertology.com 8
was great at that. He was great at pointing out that ridiculousness and recognizing it and
seeing it. But he was also great at problem solving and getting right to the heart of stuff.
Kevin: One thing that I learned about... It's not true of every single market but it's true of a lot of
savvy marketers. Savvy marketers that are good at copywriting develop an odd sense of
humor. And I think that comes from when you're a marketer you have to stop being
politically correct and you have to start understanding true human nature. And when you
start understanding true human nature you start to laugh at it all. That's one of the things.
Once you do that there's a certain thing where you kind of see what people say they are
and what they really are. And then you start to play with it and start to mess with people.
One of the things that he used to do just to mess with people because he just didn't care
anymore was the one time, I remember I was standing with him. We got into this elevator
and there was a guy and we were in Miami when all the models were down there modeling.
There's this concentration of models everywhere. Obviously, we got into this elevator, there
was a guy in the elevator and very obviously a model. The doors close and my dad turns
around and goes, "I just want to ask you all. Have you taken Jesus in your heart today?" And
this guy backs up to the wall and the girl gets it. And she started to crouch down. She's
laughing so hard you think she's going to pee in the elevator. She can't control herself from
laughing. That was his thing. Total strangers, they were not people in a sense, they were
just guinea pigs for whatever. And it wasn't just marketing, he couldn't turn off that thing.
He didn't have to have an experiment for something he was working on. He just had to
double check on human nature and stuff.
The thing about the Mustang was, in fact this subject came up once when we were talking
about I think a survey actually. Sometimes you think maybe people won't tell you the truth
and all those stuff. When it comes to what people want they will tell you exactly what they
want because they will be happy if you give that to them for what they want. My dad had
recently gotten out of Boron Federal Prison Camp and he was setting up his life again. I was
living at my mom's house at the time. He goes, "Hey Kevin. What kind of car would you
want?" "I'd like a Ferrari Testarossa." That was the car of the day back then. He's like, "No,
but within reason." But I remember it was a commercial. This commercial worked. It was
funny. I was watching the commercial. There was a girl driving this white convertible
Mustang in the hills in the 80's. I said, "A white convertible Mustang." He came back the
following day and he says, "How do you like my car?" And I went to look and it was a white
convertible Mustang. And the thing that was interesting was he asked me, because he
doesn't care about cars. He makes everybody else drive his cars. His Rolls Royce, we were
driving it. The Mustang, you name the car, we were always driving it. He didn't care about
cars. Cars were not a status symbol for him in reality. He knew at the end of the day he
could get me to do anything he wanted by telling me I can keep the car overnight if I would
go do the thing he wanted. That would be drive him home, pick him up, get his dry cleaning,
do whatever. I didn't know. I thought he wanted to be a cool kid. I thought he wanted
whatever the kids were liking because he wanted to be a younger guy. I had no idea I handed
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 13 --

Halbert Interview Crib Notes
www.halbertology.com 9
him basically control of me, hook line and sinker, by doing the one little surveying and going,
"What kind of car would you like?" He controlled me for 3 years with that car because I gave
it up. I told him the secret. It didn't dawn on me. I think it wasn't until he passed away that
I go, "Oh my god, that's what he was doing to me."
Bond: It's the way that he would teach us. If we were going somewhere to learn he'd say, "We're
going to this meeting. This is what I'm going to do. This is what I want to pitch them. This is
what I want and we'll see how it goes." We'd be in the meeting and on the way home he's
like, "Okay, did you see how so and so said this. This is what he's really going to do and this
is what this guy is thinking. Did you see where he said this? This is what he really meant and
this is where it comes from." He break that all down and stuff like that. But the only time
he was really keeping it a secret from us when we were the guinea pigs.
Kevin: There was a really good time what he was also good at. He also knew to be Machiavellian
[Unintelligible]. I think being Machiavellian to him was just what was the consequences of
being that way if it was worth risk or it wasn't going to be a problem he would do it. I
remember one time we were in LA and it was back before they broke up the phone
companies. There was just one phone company and they didn't care because if they didn't
treat you right you weren't going to go anywhere else, so you had no choice, so back then
it was like the DMV you had to do it their way or else. And it was laid out like the DMV. The
office was like a whole bunch of tables in a massive room. I remember because he had
gotten the fancy double jacks for a fax machine, so you could take your phone line and use
it both as a fax machine and a telephone without having to plug them it. You can get them
for 99 cents now. Back then this was a big deal. I remember standing outside of this place.
I even remember the location. He was like, "Watch this." He walks into this place and he
goes up to this woman and says, "I'm coming here for the thing." She goes, “We don't have
it. You can't have it." He just went ballistic. He just freaked out in this place and just made
everybody in the room incredibly uncomfortable like he was livid, like he was fuming. And
then when we walked out the door his fuming disappeared and it turned into smile and he
goes, "Watch. We're going to have that phone when I come back in an hour." He was not
mad. He knew what it took to get it done. We walked back in an hour later and now was
sitting on the desk, the thing he wanted. And he knew what he had asked himself, which a
lot of marketers should ask is, what do this people need to hear in order to buy and can you
do it ethically? Basically what does it take. If you don't have the answer you can't give that
answer and you can't do it. He always knew. He would always drill down the truth, what
does this take?
Christine: What makes this event different? Halbertology I mean. Aside from the amazing guest roster,
what makes it unique?
Bond: I personally think what's going to stand out about is usually the course that's giving you
strategy in an area. One area or another and you usually have about a top branded, well-
established expert who you knew you can listen to. And then there's some filler in there.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 13 --

Halbert Interview Crib Notes
www.halbertology.com 10
The information that you need to know but you really could look up and find in a PDF online.
What we're putting together here is not only A-listers in each of these different segments
and areas, people who can give you all the right strategies that you need to grow a business
right now. But they're doing it better than a lot of the other experts in their area. They're
not just best-selling authors, they've got several New York Times best-selling books on
there. So they're not people who are just good or even great at what they do, they're some
of the people who are basically the best in what they do.
Kevin: The interesting thing is this role has stuff that interest people but they're so broad, it's got
so many experts in so many different fields. But there's going to be other stuff that maybe
you didn't know of. One of the interesting things that successful copywriters do, the second
that we become successful is they stop being a copywriter. What they do is they hire other
copywriters but they have to be or they copy chief. What that means is that they know what
kind of mojo you have to have to write sizzling copy that will work. You can't just go, "You
can't hire copywriters.” Go ahead, pay the huge amount of money to get a copywriter when
you don't. But one of the things is that when you know how to do it yourself then you know
who to hire and how to do that, and you know what to look for and stuff like that. When it
comes to all these different experts there's things that will come into these that you will
never consider and there are things that you will be looking out for. It'll be things you'll be
watching out for, for things that are pitfalls and stuff like that, that these experts will have.
And so even if you don't do that yourself you'll be able to either know how it really works
or what great questions to ask for the right person to do it. Who are the right people to go
to. What the right services to engage in and all that stuff. There's no way that you're going
to be doing all of these stuff but you might have a team that does all this stuff, or you might
be wanting to engage in this stuff, or add more on these components to what you're doing
and scale up and do that. I think the other thing is that, or I think the keys to a program like
this is being the right person that has the right understanding, core understanding of things,
because that helps you write past these experts, the ones that we interview but the ones
that where everyone comes in and just teach is asking the right questions that don't waste
the time, that make the most use of someone's precious time that is an expert that are
coming and giving us.
I've seen it before. I remember what we did with one person and he was on and we get an
interview. I asked him this question. He goes, "Oh man, you're going to literally pull this out
on me." You could tell that he was going to give me the answer but he didn't want to give
the answer. But I knew I asked the right question. It was basically how to reverse engineer
something he was doing. He gave it up anyways and he admitted this is how it works. Being
in the right position to have the right amount of experience to ask the question that's really
relevant and get those people to answer them. Sometimes you find that we'll ask the
question that maybe you didn't think of asking.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 13 --

Halbert Interview Crib Notes
www.halbertology.com 11
Christine: Kevin, Bond, I really want to thank you for taking the time to sit down with me today. I'm
sure everyone involved with Halbertology is going to be really excited to hear everything
you shared with us. Thank you so much.
www.getwsodownload.com

materials or as a result of the services. Examples in materials are not to be interpreted as a promise or
guarantee of earnings. Earning potential is entirely dependent on the person using our product, ideas,
techniques and services. We do not purport this as a get rich quick scheme – the techniques may be proven
however their capacity to create wealth is based purely on the commitment, determination and willingness of
the individual(s) concerned.
Materials in our product and/or our website and/or services may contain information that includes, or is
based upon, forward-looking statements within the meaning of the securities litigation reform act of 1995.
Forward-looking statements give our expectations or forecasts of future events and are intended to express
our opinion of earnings potential. You can identify these statements by the fact that they do not relate strictly
to historical or current facts. Words such as “anticipate,” “project,” “estimate,” “expect,” “plan,” “intend,”
“believe,” and other words and terms of similar meaning in connection with a description of potential
earnings or financial performance might be used.
There are many factors that will be important in determining your actual results and no guarantees are made
that you will achieve results similar to ours or anybody else’s. In fact no guarantees are made that you will
achieve any results from our ideas and techniques in our material. All and earnings or income examples
provided are only estimates off what we think you could earn. At no time is there any assurance that you will
in fact also do so by replying on these figures.. We do not purport that any prior success or past results as to
income earnings are indicative of your future success or results.
Internet businesses and marketing and earnings derived there from may have unknown risks attached and
are not suitable for everyone and there is risk that one could experience significant losses or make no money
at all.
Business carries a high level of risk, and may not be suitable for all investors. Before deciding to set out on
any business venture, you should carefully consider your investment objectives, level of experience, and risk
appetite. The possibility exists that you could sustain a loss of some or all of your initial investment and
therefore, you should not invest money that you cannot afford to lose. You should be aware of all the risks
associated with a business, and seek advice if you have any doubts from an independent financial advisor
and/or qualified professionals such as lawyer or accountant before acting on any of this information.
Users of our services and/or products are advised to do their own due diligence when making business
decisions or any reliance made based on them. We are not to be held liable for the successor failure of your
decisions relating to any information or product or services presented by us to you and relied on or used by
you.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 13 --

Halbert Interview Crib Notes
www.halbertology.com 13
Your use of any information or materials on this website and/or services through it is entirely at your own
risk, for which we shall not be liable. It shall be your own responsibility to ensure that any products, services
or information available through this website and product meet your specific requirements.
When you use any information or materials provided by us, you understand that it can be outdated by the
time you use it. You also understand that in order to see results from using this information, it will require
work and could involve spending money on advertising.
This document/or materials including strategies and websites contain material which is owned by or licensed
to us and is copyright protected at all times. This material includes, but is not limited to, the design, layout,
appearance, look, videos audio and graphics, images and text. Reproduction thereof is copyright protected so
this means that it may not be copied or reproduced in any way or for any reason, or displayed on an public
domain or site is prohibited other than in accordance with the copyright notice, which forms part of our
terms and conditions. All trade marks reproduced in this website which are not the property of, or licensed
to, the operator are acknowledged on the website. You will receive certain material and verbal and written
communications and agree to hold any such information of any sort confidential at all times.
Unauthorized use of this website and or material may give rise to a claim for damages and/or be a criminal
offense.
We would never mean to say or pretend that any person will or is likely to achieve profits or loses similar to
those discussed on this website. Each person may have different results. In fact, it’s pretty certain they and
you will not have similar results and there are frequently sharp differences between hypothetical
performance results and actual results subsequently achieved by any particular business. So the saying "Past
performance is not indicative of future results" really holds true here.
One of the limitations of hypothetical performance results is that they are generally prepared with the benefit
of hindsight. Also, hypothetical trading does not involve financial risk and that’s why no hypothetical profit
record can completely account for the impact of financial risk in business.
There are a lot of other factors related to the market in general or to the implementation of any specific
business objective which cannot be fully accounted for in the preparation of hypothetical performance results
and all of which can adversely affect results.
Although we make every attempt to assure accuracy, we do not give any express or implied warranty as to its
accuracy. What we are saying here is that we do not accept any liability for any error or omission. You'll see
we have examples posted now and again. These are for illustrative purposes only so please do not be
construe them as investment advice or strategy.
Halbertology (dot) com and (dot) net, Mark McRae, Kevin Halbert and Bond Halbert, the website and its
representatives including product developers and authors and guest speakers do not and cannot give
investment advice or invite customers to engage in investments through this website.
It follows then also that we and anybody else who helped us create, produce, or deliver the site and any
materials bought by you are not liable for any damages you suffer if and when and however you use it/them.
We or any of these persons mentioned above, are never to be held liable by you for any: "direct, incidental,
consequential, indirect, or punitive damages arising out of your access to, or use of, the site or services. or
product or inability to do so either in whole or in part. Without limiting the foregoing, everything on the site
and via services is provided to you 'AS IS' WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EITHER EXPRESSED OR
IMPLIED, INCLUDING, BUT NOT LIMITED TO, THE IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS
FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, OR NON INFRINGEMENT."
By becoming a member of our website or using it or our services, you have agreed that you unconditionally
accept this disclaimer agreement as outlined and read with our terms of use and privacy policy.
